title: An Application of a Conjecture of Mazur-Tate to Supersingular Elliptic Curves
speaker: Emmanuel Lecouturier
abstract: In 1987, Barry Mazur and John Tate formulated refined conjectures of the "Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer type", and one of these conjectures was essentially proved in the prime conductor case by Ehud de Shalit in 1995. One of the main objects in de Shalit's work is the so-called refined $\mathscr{L}$ invariant, which happens to be a Hecke operator. We apply some results of the theory of Mazur's Eisenstein ideal to study in which power of the Eisenstein ideal $\mathscr{L}$ belongs.

proof_stages.label: Exceptional-zero formula in modular-symbol form
proof_stages.summary: The talk recalls modular symbols attached to an elliptic curve, the plus symbol used to construct the p-adic L-function, the Tate-curve L-invariant in the split multiplicative case, and the congruence version of the Mazur-Tate-Teitelbaum/Greenberg-Stevens exceptional-zero formula.

proof_stages.label: Greenberg-Stevens proof template
proof_stages.summary: The proof strategy for the p-adic formula is isolated as two ingredients: a Hida-family two-variable p-adic L-function relating the derivative to the weight derivative of a_p, and a Galois-deformation argument relating that derivative to the L-invariant.

proof_stages.label: Finite-level Mazur-Tate modular elements
proof_stages.summary: The speaker passes from the p-adic congruences to Mazur-Tate's finite-level, exponentiated formulation, introduces modular elements theta_{E,m} in group rings and their interpolation property, reformulates the conjecture via augmentation ideals, and recalls de Shalit's partial conductor-p theorem.

proof_stages.label: Uniformization of J0(p) and the operator L
proof_stages.summary: To state the Mazur-Tate analogue for the Jacobian J0(p), the talk replaces the Tate uniformization by the p-adic uniformization coming from the supersingular special fiber, defines the pairing q on the supersingular module X, constructs the logarithm-over-valuation endomorphism L, and recalls de Shalit's explicit formula for L in terms of logarithms of differences of supersingular j-invariants.

proof_stages.label: Mazur-Tate identity for all level-p modular symbols
proof_stages.summary: The main finite-level identity is stated: for every weight-two level-Gamma_0(p) modular symbol phi, applying the Hecke operator L and evaluating at {0,infinity} equals the discrete-log weighted Mazur-Tate sum over a/p, extending de Shalit's result from an Eisenstein subspace to all modular symbols.

proof_stages.label: Eisenstein-square containment
proof_stages.summary: After recalling Mazur's Eisenstein ideal and the congruence between cusp forms and the Eisenstein series, the speaker uses the modular-symbol identity to show that the Hecke operator L lies in the square of the Eisenstein ideal modulo l.

proof_stages.label: Consequences for supersingular elliptic curves
proof_stages.summary: The containment L in I^2 implies that the kernel of L on the mod-l supersingular module has dimension at least two, containing the Eisenstein and higher Eisenstein elements, and combining this with de Shalit's j-invariant formula yields an explicit weighted-tree identity among supersingular j-invariants.

proof_stages.label: Cubic Eisenstein question and K2 criterion
proof_stages.summary: The talk closes by asking when L lies in the cube of the Eisenstein ideal and gives a criterion, conditional on a Sharifi-type conjecture, in terms of a Steinberg-symbol element in K2 of the p-th cyclotomic ring lying in the square of the augmentation ideal.

examples.name: Lambda-invariant variant of the de Shalit/Oesterle formula
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker mentions replacing the supersingular j-invariant by the modular lambda invariant as another instance of such formulas, illustrating their broader modular-curve scope without being used later.
examples.engagement_reason: The lambda-invariant variant is only cited as an analogous version obtained by replacing the j-invariant, without writing or using the variant formula.

passes.reason: A lambda-invariant analogue of the de Shalit/Oesterlé formula is mentioned as an ordinary variant/generalization, not as a classification exception or refutation.
The lambda-invariant replacement is cited as an ordinary analogue/generalization of the de Shalit--Oesterlé formula, not as a special limiting object or failure case.
a lambda-invariant analogue of the de Shalit/Oesterlé formula is presented as a parallel variant, not as a sporadic, limiting, optimal, or adversarial case
A lambda-invariant analogue of the de Shalit/Oesterlé supersingular formula is presented as a generalization, not as a structurally special object or a failure case.

The lambda-invariant analogue of the de Shalit/Oesterle supersingular formula is used as a parallel/generalized formula, not to refute a claim, and has no sporadic, degenerate, extremal, or maximal-symmetry structure.
It is a systematic analogue of the de Shalit/Oesterle formula obtained by replacing the j-invariant with the modular lambda invariant, not a sporadic or adversarial case.

The lambda-invariant version is cited as a parallel analogue/generalization of the de Shalit–Oesterlé formula, not as a failure case, and the lambda modular invariant is not intrinsically extremal, degenerate, maximally symmetric, or sporadic-exceptional.
An analog of the de Shalit–Oesterlé formula with the modular lambda invariant replacing the j-invariant is cited as a systematic variant, not as a disproof or structurally special object.
the lambda-invariant replacement is used as a parallel variant of the de Shalit/Oesterlé formula, not to refute anything, and is not structurally sporadic or extremal

A lambda-invariant analogue of the de Shalit/Oesterlé supersingular formula is cited as a parallel result, not as a failure case, and it is part of a systematic family of such modular-invariant variants.
The lambda-invariant version is cited as an analogous/generalized formula, not as a failure case, and it is a standard modular-function variant rather than a structurally special object.

A lambda-invariant analogue of the de Shalit/Oesterle formula is used as a parallel generalization, not as a failure case, and is not structurally sporadic, limiting, extremal, or maximally symmetric.

examples.name: Three supersingular labels and their three trees
examples.note: The speaker specializes the weighted-tree identity to the case of three supersingular elliptic curves, where the three labeled trees make the Kirchhoff-type sum of products of logarithms of j-invariant differences concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker specializes to three supersingular curves, enumerates the three possible labeled trees, and explains the resulting sum of products of logarithmic j-invariant differences equals zero.

passes.reason: The three labeled trees on three supersingular labels are used as a small illustrative instance of the spanning-tree identity, with no special exceptional or adversarial role.
The three labeled trees are a small illustrative instance of the tree-sum formula, with no structural exceptionalness and no counterexample role.
the three labeled trees are just a small illustrative instance of the general tree-sum formula, not used to disprove anything
The three labeled trees on three supersingular labels are a small illustrative instance of the spanning-tree sum, not deployed against a claim.
The three labeled trees on three supersingular labels are a small illustrative ordinary case of the labeled-tree/Kirchhoff-type sum, not an obstruction or structurally exceptional object.
The three labelled supersingular vertices and their three labelled trees form a small illustrative Cayley/Kirchhoff case, but not a structurally collapsed or counterexample case.
The three labeled supersingular vertices and their three labeled trees are used as a minimal illustrative case of the tree-sum formula, not to refute a claim.
The case of three supersingular labels and the three possible labeled trees is used as a minimal illustrative case of the general tree-sum formula, not as a counterexample.
the three labeled trees on three supersingular labels are just a small illustrative instance of the tree-sum formula, not an obstruction or special classification exception

The three labeled supersingular vertices and their three spanning trees are used as a small illustration of the tree-sum formula, with the complete three-vertex configuration carrying the usual full relabeling symmetry.
The three-label tree picture is used as the smallest illustrative nontrivial case of the spanning-tree identity, not as a counterexample.
The three-label tree picture is a small illustrative instance of the general labeled-tree sum, not a counterexample and not intrinsically exceptional, extremal, degenerate, or maximally symmetric.
